coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent immunosensor for highly sensitive detection of leucomalachite green in fish and shrimp samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yoohanngoh, Rattawan
Wechakorn, Kanokorn
Mansalai, Preecha
Teepoo, Siriwan
coredata.dc:description: This study developed a highly sensitive fluorescent immunosensor using carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescent label. The immunosensor was effectively employed to detect leucomalachite green (LMG) in fish and shrimp samples. The CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal procedure and have demonstrated an excellent fluorescence signal with a small particle size of 4.3 nm. The CDs were covalently bonded with the antibody of LMG and utilized as a fluorescent probe. The results exhibited a linear correlation in the decrease of fluorescence intensity within the range of 0.01–4.0 ng/mL (r = 0.9999). The low detection limit of 0.0082 ng/mL was achieved. The proposed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor demonstrated excellent specificity, accuracy, and sensitivity toward LMG. The assay accurately detected LMG in fish and shrimp samples with a high level of accuracy (recovery = 95 %-105 %). Based on these results, the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor demonstrated high sensitivity, specificity, and stability, making it suitable for detecting LMG in fish and shrimp samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Leucomalachite green
Fluorescent immunosensor
Carbon dots
Fish
Shrimp

CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor response to LMG

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by The Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Funding (TSRI) (Grant no. FRB650070/0168). This research block grant was managed under Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi (FRB65E0609).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the principle of CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor for LMG detection.

TEM image of CDs (a), CDs@anti-LMG (b), AFM results of CDs (c), CDs@anti-LMG (d), XRD result of CDs (e), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of CDs (f) (insert: photos of CDs under (i) visible light and (ii) 365 nm UV light), UV/visible absorption spectra (g), fluorescence spectra (h), FT-IR spectra (i) of the CDs and CDs@anti-LMG, and UV/visible absorption spectra of BSA, LMG, and LMG@BSA (j).

Fluorescence intensity of immunosensor response to serials concentrations of LMG (0 – 4.0 ng/mL) under conditions using 0.8 mg/mL CDs@anti-LMG, 0.5 mg/mL LMG@BSA, 12 h for incubation time of LMG@BSA, pH 7.40, and 40 min for binding time. The inset shows the corresponding imaging under UV light. The inset shows the corresponding imaging under UV light.
Optimization of concentration of CDs@anti-LMG (a), the concentration of LMG@BSA (b), incubation time of LMG@BSA (c), pH (d), and binding time (e).

Calibration curve for LMG detection using the optimal conditions (inset: the linear range from 0.01–4.0 ng/mL LMG) (a). Fluorescence responses of the fabricated immunosensor for interferents, MG, CV, BG, Hg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, and sodium benzoate at the same concentration of LMG at 2.0 ng/mL (b) Under conditions using 0.5 mg/mL CDs@anti-LMG, 0.6 mg/mL LMG@BSA, 12 h for incubation time of LMG@BSA, pH 7.40, and 30 min for binding time.
Comparison of different analytical methods for LMG detection.

row.entry: Simultaneous detection of MG and LMG
Simultaneous detection of MG and LMG
Simultaneous detection of MG and LMG

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ppb = 1 part per billion (ng/g, ng/mL, µg/kg)
LC - VIS/FLD = Liquid chromatography with visible and fluorescence
LC - MS/MS = Liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry
Direct ELISA = Direct enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Direct Competitive ELISA = Direct competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LMG detection results in real samples using the CDs-based fluorescence immunosensor, ELISA and HPLC-MS/MS.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent immunosensor for highly sensitive detection of leucomalachite green in fish and shrimp samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi, Thanyaburi, Pathum Thani 12110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a highly sensitive fluorescent immunosensor using carbon dots (CDs) as a fluorescent label. The immunosensor was effectively employed to detect leucomalachite green (LMG) in fish and shrimp samples. The CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal procedure and have demonstrated an excellent fluorescence signal with a small particle size of 4.3 nm. The CDs were covalently bonded with the antibody of LMG and utilized as a fluorescent probe. The results exhibited a linear correlation in the decrease of fluorescence intensity within the range of 0.01–4.0 ng/mL (r = 0.9999). The low detection limit of 0.0082 ng/mL was achieved. The proposed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor demonstrated excellent specificity, accuracy, and sensitivity toward LMG. The assay accurately detected LMG in fish and shrimp samples with a high level of accuracy (recovery = 95 %-105 %). Based on these results, the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor demonstrated high sensitivity, specificity, and stability, making it suitable for detecting LMG in fish and shrimp samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Leucomalachite green (LMG) is a dominant derivative of malachite green (MG) that is frequently utilized in many industries (De Freitas et al., 2024; Dong et al., 2021). MG has been extensively utilized as an inexpensive, readily available, and highly effective antifungal and antibacterial agent in the aquaculture field, especially in fish farming (Akram et al., 2023; Deng et al., 2021). MG is readily absorbed by aquatic animals in their edible tissues and is subsequently converted to LMG in organisms, and LMG has a longer half-life and greater toxicity in the human body compared to MG (Gharavi-Nakhjavani et al., 2023; Cao et al., 2024). It has been established that LMG is genotoxic to human DNA, teratogenic, carcinogenic, and mutagenic (Pandey et al., 2022; Yue et al., 2022). As a result, several countries prohibit the use of LMG in aquatic products (Kwan et al., 2020; Pipoyan et al., 2020). For LMG and MG in aquatic goods, the European Union has established a minimum required performance limit (MRPL) of 2 µg/kg (Glynn et al., 2021). As a result, it is necessary to establish a sensitive and precise method for detecting LMG residues in aquatic products.

The analysis methods commonly employed for the detection are high-performance liquid chromatography-photodiode array (HPLC-PDA) (Jiang et al., 2020), high-performance liquid chromatography-visible detection (HPLC–VIS) (Pandey et al., 2022), high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) (Kaplan et al., 2014), liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) (dos Santos Viana et al., 2021), surface-enhanced resonance Raman scattering (Qin et al., 2024), enzyme-linked immunosorbent test (ELISA) (Xing et al., 2009), and immunosensors (Dong et al., 2021). The immunosensor is attracting significant interest because of its major advantages, including high specificity, high accuracy, simplicity, and rapid detection (Li et al., 2020; You et al., 2021; Ziyang et al., 2025). The detection of LMG on the immunosensor mainly relies on the competitive immunoassay technique. Fluorescent signals offer significant advantages compared to colorimetric signals in terms of high sensitivity, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness for the fabrication of immunosensors (Sargazi et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of carbon nanostructure that provide many significant benefits, such as highly tuned photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, a simple production procedure, few toxicities, and environmental friendliness (Zhang et al., 2024; Goswami et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2023). CDs have shown their capabilities in chemical sensing (Carneiro et al., 2023), bio-sensing (Khan et al., 2022), and drug delivery (Mohammed et al., 2023) in recent years. CDs can be easily conjugated with antibodies, producing a high fluorescence intensity signal, which enables low-cost and large-scale production during synthesis. This makes CDs a powerful tool for use as fluorescence labels in immunosensor platforms. Wang et al. (2024) developed a fluorescent immunosensor based on CDs to detect E. coli O157:H7 in food samples. The CDs demonstrated fluorescence restoration and achieved a limit of detection of 7 CFU/mL. Nevertheless, there is currently no research on the application of CDs as fluorescent probes in a fluorescent immunosensor for the detection of LMG.

Thus, this study aimed to develop a highly sensitive fluorescent immunosensor, depicted in Fig. 1, for detecting trace amounts of LMG in aquatic animal samples for the first time. The immunosensor utilized CDs as a labeling probe (CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor). The analytical conditions were optimized, and the performance of the proposed test was evaluated. Additionally, the practicality of using this method on actual fish and shrimp samples was confirmed and compared to the ELISA and HPLC-MS/MS.

The LMG polyclonal antibody (anti-LMG) was obtained from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). The following chemicals were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Louis, USA): LMG, 4-formylbenzoic acid, N, N-dimethylaniline, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamino propyl) carbodiimide (EDC), hydroxy succinimide (NHS), and ethylenediamine. A Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave was obtained from Baoshishan (New York, USA). The fluorescence response was recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FMP-825 with FP-8350/8550, Tokyo, Japan). The surface and chemical properties of the synthesized CDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEM-1400, Tokyo, Japan), a UV–visible spectrophotometer (C-7000V, South Africa), an X-ray diffractometer (XRD; PW3040/60, Kaki Bukit, Singapore), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Bruker, Massachusetts, USA), and atomic force microscopes (AFM; Park NX10, park systems pte. ltd., Singapore). HPLC-MS/MS was obtained from Agilent Technologies (Agilent 6495, Agilent Technologies, Inc, USA).

CDs were prepared from citric acid and ethylenediamine via a hydrothermal reaction. Citric acid (3.15 g) and ethylenediamine (1 mL) were mixed in deionized water (DI water) and then heated in an oven at 473 K for 5 h. After that, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. The obtained mixture was filtrated via filter paper and a 0.2 µm membrane filter. Then, the CD solution was dialyzed in DI water for 12 h to remove any unreacted materials or small molecular impurities. The purified solution is then freeze-dried overnight, yielding the final CDs in solid form.

A functionalization reaction was performed to modify carboxyl functional groups onto CDs. The as-synthesized CDs (100 mg) were dissolved in DI water (50 mL). 5 g of sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and 5 g of sodium chloroacetate (ClCH2COONa) were added to the CDs solution and then sonicated at 900 W for 6 h until the color of the solution changed from yellow to saffron. After that, the reaction mixture was adjusted to pH 7.00 using 10 % w/v HCl and dialyzed in DI water for 12 h to remove any excess reagents and byproducts. Finally, the purified product is freeze-dried overnight, yielding carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (CDs-COOH).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent coupling of CDs and anti-LMG (CDs@anti-LMG)
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, EDC (1 mg) and NHS (1 mg) were dissolved in CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 1 mL). The main aim of EDC/NHS activation of the carboxylic acids of CDs is to enable covalent bonding with free amine-containing anti-LMG through coupling. The mixture was subsequently stirred at an ambient temperature for 10 min. Afterward, 100 µL of a 0.1 µg/mL anti-LMG was added, and the mixture was stirred continuously for 1 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 277 K and 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting CDs@anti-LMG were then collected and kept at 277 K for future use.‬

The preparation of COOH-LMG was carried out following previous studies, with some modifications (Yang et al., 2007). In summary, a mixture was obtained by adding 450 mg of 4-formylbenzoic acid, 1.2 mL of N,N-dimethylaniline, and 1.2 g of zinc chloride. Afterward, the mixture was left to reflux for a period of 24 h at 363 K. After being cooled to room temperature, 30 mL of methanol was added. After adjusting the solution’s pH to 5.00, a green solid was obtained. The solid was then filtrated, followed by washing with DI water, and subsequently dried at 353 K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent coupling of COOH-LMG and BSA (LMG@BSA)
ce:section.ce:para: A solution containing 4 mL of a phosphate buffer with a pH of 5.00, COOH-LMG (20 mg), BSA (8 mg), and EDC (240 mg) was prepared. BSA was used as a protein carrier for the coating antigen on the microplate wells. After that, the mixture was continuously stirred for 2 h at 277 K. Then, this solution was dialyzed against a 0.1 M phosphate buffer (pH 5.00) at 277 K for 2 days to produce LMG@BSA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of LMG from real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The 20 dead samples (fish and shrimp) were bought at the local market. The extraction procedure was modified from previous studies (Teepoo et al., 2020). First, 1 g of each sample was mixed with 4 mL of acetonitrile and homogenized for 5 min. The solution was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. These steps were repeated twice, and all the supernatants were combined. The resulting supernatant (1 mL) was then used for analysis with the proposed fluorescent immunosensor.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic representation of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor procedures. The microplate wells were coated with a solution of 0.1 M LMG@BSA (100 µL) in a carbonate buffer with a pH of 9.60. The coating procedure was conducted at 277 K for a period of 12 h. The LMG@BSA was effectively maintained by washing with a washing buffer (0.1 M phosphate buffer at pH 7.40 containing 1 % w/v of BSA). The extracted sample, or LMG standard solution, was added to the CDs@anti-LMG solution. This mixture was added to the wells. Following a 30 min incubation period, a competitive binding reaction was formed. The excess unbound CDs@anti-LMG were eliminated using a washing buffer. Afterward, 200 µL of a 0.1 M phosphate buffer was introduced to each well. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Three duplicates of the analysis were performed. The fluorescence signal was plotted against the concentration of LMG to create the calibration curves for quantitative analysis of LMG. The linear equation derived from the calibration curve was subsequently employed to determine the LMG concentration in real samples.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Characterization of CDs, CDs@anti-LMG, and LMG@BSA

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CDs from citric acid and ethylenediamine follows a bottom-up hydrothermal carbonization mechanism, which involves dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization (Zhang et al., 2023; Vervald et al., 2025). The carboxyl-functionalized carbon dots (CDs-COOH) were prepared by surface functionalization using sodium chloroacetate under alkaline conditions via a nucleophilic substitution mechanism. The shape and size distribution of CDs were determined using TEM. CDs are well-monodispersed with an average diameter of 4.3 nm and a uniformly spherical shape, as shown in Fig. 2a. After conjugation of anti-LMG to CDs, the anti-LMG binds covalently to the CDs, forming loose nanoconjugates with an increased size of 79.2 nm (Fig. 2b).

AFM was employed to examine the surface topography of CDs and CDs@anti-LMG. As shown in Fig. 2c, the three-dimensional AFM image of the bare CDs revealed a relatively flat surface with a roughness average value (Ra) of 12 nm. Following the conjugation of anti-LMG, a dense bump-like structure appeared on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2d), resulting in an increased Ra of 34 nm. This increase in roughness indicates successful anti-LMG loading on the surface of CDs.

Fig. 2e displays the results of the XRD studies. The peaks of CDs observed at 2θ = 22.7 degrees can be explained by the crystal plane of graphite (002) (Abidin et al., 2021). The XRD results indicated that the CDs were successfully prepared. Subsequently, the optical characteristics of CDs were studied. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 2f It demonstrates that the wavelengths at which the highest fluorescence intensity occurs are 340 nm for excitation and 446 nm for emission. The insets in Fig. 2f exhibit a yellow color (i) under visible light, while appearing light blue (ii) when exposed to 365 nm UV light, indicating the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the solution.Then, the CDs@anti-LMG preparation was carried out via carbodiimide coupling reaction. Fig. 2g displays the UV/visible absorption spectra of the CDs and CDs@anti-LMG. Compared to CDs, CDs@anti-LMG conjugates have a clearly new absorption peak at 274 nm resulting from anti-LMG. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@anti-LMG decreased due to the coating with anti-LMG on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2h). The UV/visible and fluorescence spectra characterization confirmed that CDs were successfully conjugated to anti-LMG.‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬‬ Furthermore, the conjugation of anti-LMG to CDs was confirmed through the FT-IR spectra. Fig. 2i shows that the FT-IR spectra of CDs@anti-LMG showed typical absorption peaks between 1602 and 1481 cm−1, corresponding to the protein amide bands I and II, confirming the successful conjugation of anti-LMG to the surface of CDs (Ji et al., 2020).

BSA conjugated with LMG was characterized by a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Fig. 2j). For LMG@BSA, within the range of 250–1000 nm, BSA exhibits a characteristic peak at 278 nm, while LMG shows a peak at 620 nm. The prepared LMG@BSA displayed the characteristic absorption peaks of both BSA and LMG, confirming its successful formation.
All results confirmed that CDs@anti-LMG and LMG@BSA were successfully conjugated and are now ready to be employed for a CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor to detect LMG.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized CDs was studied, and the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) was measured. The QY of the CDs was found to be 3.23 % (Fig. S1). Fluorescence stability is crucial for CDs, as it is a key requirement for their practical applications. In this study, fluorescence stability performance was evaluated under different conditions: (1) exposure to a specific radiant intensity of UV irradiation, (2) chemical stability, and (3) storage stability. The photostability of the synthesized CDs and CDs@anti-LMG was studied under UV irradiation at 365 nm. The photostability results of the synthesized CDs are presented in Fig. S2. The results show that the fluorescence intensity remained above 99 % for CDs and 90 % for CDs@anti-LMG after continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp for 120 min.

The chemical stability of the synthesized CDs was also evaluated in varying concentrations of NaCl (0–1 M) to assess the effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S3, no significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing NaCl concentrations.

In addition, the storage stability of the synthesized CDs and CDs@anti-LMG at 277 K was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of CDs maintained 83 % of its original value, whereas CDs@anti-LMG maintained 80 % after 30 days.

These results indicate that the synthesized CDs and CDs@anti-LMG exhibited excellent photostability and chemical stability, making them suitable for use as fluorescent probes in the proposed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors, even under conditions of irradiation, strong ionic strength, and storage stability.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor response to LMG

ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor was developed using the specific interaction between LMG and anti-LMG, with CDs serving as fluorescent labels. The detection method is illustrated in Fig. 1. First, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of CDs was examined. The CDs showed a significant emission peak at 446 nm. The CDs were then used as a label probe for the fabrication of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor to monitor LMG. The CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor was based on a competitive format. The LMG present in the sample competes with LMG@BSA coated on the solid support to bind with CDs@anti-LMG. If the sample was highly contaminated with LMG, the LMG molecules in the sample solution would completely prevent the binding of CDs@anti-LMG to the LMG@BSA coating on the well, resulting in a low fluorescence signal. Fig. 3 demonstrated that, as the concentration of LMG increased, the amount of CDs@anti-LMG bound to immobilized LMG@BSA decreased. This resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity signal.

The decrease in fluorescence response exhibited an inverse correlation with the concentration of LMG, which ranged from 0.01 to 4.0 ng/mL. Under UV radiation with a wavelength of 365 nm, a color gradient from light blue to dark blue was observed as the LMG concentration increased (inset in Fig. 3).

Significant variables affecting the sensitivity of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor were optimized for detection of 2.0 ng/mL LMG, such as the concentration of CDs@anti-LMG, the concentration of LMG@BSA, the incubation time of LMG@BSA, and the binding time. The effects of anti-LMG@CD concentrations on the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor response were investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity reduced as the concentration of CDs@anti-LMG increased (0.1–1.0 mg/mL). The lowest intensity was achieved with CDs@anti-LMG at 0.5 mg/mL, indicating the strongest competitive immunoreaction between free CDs@anti-LMG and immobilized LMG@BSA. As a result, the optimal CDs@anti-LMG concentration was 0.5 mg/mL.

The optimal concentration of LMG@BSA should be determined due to its binding ability to CDs@anti-LMG and generate the fluorescence signal. The effect of LMG@BSA was examined over a concentration range of 0.3–1.0 mg/mL for the detection of 2.0 ng/mL LMG, and the results are shown in Fig. 4b. The signal response of the LMG reduced as the concentration of LMG@BSA increased. However, it reached a stable signal at a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL LMG@BSA, indicating that the binding site on the well surface was saturated. So, 0.6 mg/mL of LMG@BSA was chosen as the optimum concentration.

The optimal time of incubation for LMG@BSA on the well was studied from 3 to 15 h, and the optimized incubation time was found to be 12 h (Fig. 4c). As a result, the optimum incubation time of 12 h was selected for the next experiments.

The effect of varying pH levels on the immunosensor response was investigated. Six different pH values were studied: 7.00, 7.20, 7.40, 7.60, 7.80, and 8.00. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence signal decreased between pH 7.00 and pH 7.40 and then stabilized. Therefore, pH 7.40 was selected for subsequent research.

The binding time between LMG@BSA and CDs@anti-LMG was also optimized by varying from 10 to 50 min. Fig. 4e shows the binding time optimization results. The fluorescence response of the CDs-based completed immunosensor at 30 min was close to the equilibrium value, indicating the completion of the immunoreaction. Therefore, the optimum time for binding was set at 30 min.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Analytical performance of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear range, limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ)
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor was evaluated by studying the linear range, LOD, and LOQ. As the concentration of LMG increased, the intensity of the fluorescence gradually decreased. The fluorescence intensity values were measured and plotted as a function of the concentrations of LMG, as depicted in Fig. 5a. Fig. 5a (inset) shows the linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and LMG concentration (y = -23.765x + 109.13; r = 0.9999). This correlation is observed within the concentration range of 0.01–4.0 ng/mL. The LOD was determined using the formula LOD = 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 10), and represents the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD was determined to be 0.0082 ng/mL. The LOQ was calculated to be 0.028 ng/mL using the 10σ/s formula. In comparison to the results presented in the literature ( Table 1), the new CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor has a wider linear detection range with a low concentration range. This indicates that the immunosensor has great potential for application and a lower LOD due to its excellent optical properties as a fluorescent probe.
Moreover, the LOQ of the developed method is significantly lower than the MRPL of LMG in aquaculture samples.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the established immunosensor were also evaluated. The repeatability of detecting LMG at a concentration of 2.0 ng/mL was determined by carrying out 7 repeated assays on the same day. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) value was 1.9 %. To examine the reproducibility of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor, a total of 7 immunosensors, each fabricated in an individual batch, were employed to detect a concentration of 2.0 ng/mL LMG. The RSD was found to be 2.9 %, and the experimental results demonstrated that the established CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors exhibited good repeatability and reproducibility. Moreover, an inter-laboratory comparison was conducted to assess the reproducibility of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor results, with five different laboratories conducting the detection of 2.0 ng/mL LMG. The RSD of the measurements was 3.2 %. This demonstrates the excellent precision of the developed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor.

The other important key factor for application in the real sample was selectivity. The specificity of the proposed assay was assessed by evaluating its selectivity against various organic dyes, including MG, crystal violet (CV), and brilliant green (BG), as well as heavy metals such as mercury ion (Hg2+), cadmium ion (Cd2+), nickel ion (Ni2+), along with the preservative sodium benzoate. Fig. 5b demonstrates that only the LMG was able to induce a significant change in fluorescence intensity, whereas other interfering substances had no effect on the fluorescence signal, similar to the blank result. This indicates that the designed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor exhibits a high selectivity for detecting LMG.

The 30-day storage stability study demonstrated a significant impact on sensitivity. The proposed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor was stored at 277 K for 30 days under two conditions: long-term light exposure and dark storage. Subsequently, the immunosensor was used to detect LMG at a concentration of 2.0 ng/mL. The daily immunosensor test results are shown in Fig. S5. These results indicated that the immunosensor's responses did not change significantly over the storage period. Even after 30 days, the proposed immunosensor maintained remarkable stability, retaining approximately 89 % of its initial value under both conditions, likely due to the strong covalent bonding of the CDs-antibody conjugates. These results indicate that the developed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors exhibit good stability when stored at 277 K under both light and dark conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LMG in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Before the analysis of LMG in the real sample, the matrix effect was evaluated. To test the matrix effect in a real sample, matrix-matched calibration curves were prepared by spiking blank sample (snakehead fish) with 0.7–2 ng/mL of LMG solution. Matrix effects were quantified by comparing analytical response of an analyte in a solvent to the response of the same analyte in the real sample matrix and using the formula: % matrix effect = [(Sm/Ss) − 1] × 100, where Sm is the slope of the calibration curve in the matrix and Ss is the slope in the solvent. As shown in Table S1, the matrix effect was calculated to be −1 %, which is within the range considered negligible (Ferrer et al., 2011). This confirms that the proposed CDs-based fluorescence immunosensor is effective for quantifying LMG in food products without requiring additional sample clean-up steps.

Next, the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors were used for the LMG analysis in fish and shrimp samples (20 samples). The concentrations of LMG were analyzed by the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor, ELISA, and HPLC-MS/MS. ELISA and HPLC-MS/MS were used as reference methods. Recovery studies were also carried out. A spiked sample was prepared by adding a known amount of LMG to the real sample matrix before any pretreatment. The analytical results from the proposed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors, ELISA, and HPLC-MS/MS are shown in Table 2. The values obtained from the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors were not significantly different from those obtained from the ELISA and HPLC-MS/MS, as confirmed by the statistical paired t-test at a 95 % confidence level. The proposed method achieves recoveries ranging from 95 % to 105 % (SD < 2.6) in the tested samples, demonstrating high accuracy and precision for use as a quantitative method to detect LMG in real samples. Compared to the conventional HPLC-MS/MS method, the developed CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor offers several key advantages: (1) significantly higher sensitivity, (2) superior specificity due to precise antibody-antigen recognition, and (3) cost-effectiveness, with an assay cost of just $10 versus $90 for HPLC-MS/MS. These results suggest that it could be used as an alternative testing method for monitoring LMG contamination in real samples.

This study established a fluorescent immunosensor to detect LMG. CDs were employed as fluorescent probes in immunosensor. The competitive fluorescent immunosensor with CDs probes demonstrated an LOD of 0.0082 ng/mL, indicating excellent detection quality compared to alternative methods for analyzing LMG, particularly as a highly sensitive assay. The LOD and LOQ were determined to be 0.0082 ng/mL and 0.028 ng/mL, respectively. The RSD of the CDs-based fluorescent immunosensor in fish and shrimp samples ranged from 0.1 % to 2.6 %, with a recovery rate of 95–105 %, demonstrating its suitability for detecting LMG in real samples. Furthermore, the proposed test was effectively validated for LMG detection in real samples, exhibiting results that were correlated with those obtained by ELISA and HPLC-MS/MS. The results showed an advantageous use of these novel CDs-based fluorescent immunosensors for detecting LMG, offering outstanding sensitivity, good selectivity, and low cost. Future research should focus on developing a field-based assay format, such as an immunochromatographic strip test.

Yoohanngoh Rattawan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Mansalai Preecha: Methodology. Wechakorn Kanokorn: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Teepoo Siriwan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Resources, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by The Science, Research and Innovation Promotion Funding (TSRI) (Grant no. FRB650070/0168). This research block grant was managed under Rajamangala University of Technology Thanyaburi (FRB65E0609).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2025.107769.
